name: Divine Authority
shortDescription: Divine authority in Judaism refers to the power and legitimacy granted by God to individuals or objects, enabling them to act on His behalf. It represents a connection to the divine source and underscores the belief that certain actions or pronouncements carry the weight of God's will.
description: Divine authority in Jewish tradition is a cornerstone of religious belief and practice. It manifests as the power and legitimacy bestowed upon individuals, objects, or institutions to act as intermediaries between God and humanity. This authority is not inherent but rather granted by God, signifying His endorsement and sanction. Moses, for example, possessed divine authority to lead the Israelites out of Egypt, evidenced by the miracles he performed through God's power. The staff of Moses is a physical representation of this authority, transforming into a serpent and parting the Red Sea, demonstrating God's power operating through Moses. Similarly, Aaron, Moses' brother, held authority as the High Priest, a role divinely ordained and central to the sacrificial rituals in the Tabernacle and later the Temple. The concept of divine authority extends to the Torah itself, which is considered the revealed word of God and the ultimate source of Jewish law and ethics. The prophets also spoke with divine authority, delivering God's messages and warnings to the people. This authority is not absolute; it is always subject to God's will and purpose. The Sanhedrin, the high court in ancient Israel, derived its authority from its interpretation and application of the Torah. The legitimacy of religious leaders and institutions rests on their adherence to God's commandments and their ability to guide the community in accordance with His will. The burning bush serves as a powerful symbol of God's initial bestowal of divine authority upon Moses, commissioning him for his divinely appointed task. This underscores that divine authority is not self-assumed but rather a direct mandate from God.

sources.title: The Jewish Study Bible
sources.author: Adele Berlin and Marc Zvi Brettler
